rationale.R1.2: The artifact contains at least two internal contradictions that make logical consistency load-bearing under almost any reading of 'check this.' First, the cost-savings paragraph inverts the routing logic: it states the 70B model is reserved 'for queries the gating classifier marks as easy,' which directly contradicts the Section 3 policy (s ≥ τ → Tier-2 = hard queries). Second, the latency paragraph reports p99 rising from 412 ms to 587 ms under FROST, then declares this 'a substantial improvement in tail responsiveness' — a factually backward interpretation of the numbers. The Pareto-improvement conclusion downstream of these two errors is therefore unsupported.
rationale.R1.3: The headline claims — 41% cost reduction and improved tail latency — are the empirical load-bearing assertions of the paper. The latency number (587 ms > 412 ms) directly falsifies the improvement claim rather than supporting it, making evidence-claim alignment a central responsibility regardless of whether the user wants a deep logical audit or a lighter pass.

parsed.clarification_question: Should 'check this' target substantive correctness (internal logical consistency and whether reported numbers actually support the stated conclusions), or writing and grammar quality, or both?
